description: To 'napako' is to cook food through surrounding dry heat, often inside an enclosed heated place. Bread is the familiar case, but roots, fruit, dough, and other foods also fit. 'therapi' (boil) works through moving liquid; baking lets heat reach the food from the chamber around it.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for voiced dental 'n', carry the sound through the nose, and open into central 'a'. Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release into stressed central 'a'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k', then finish on true-mid rounded 'o'.
sound_symbolism: The opening sounds 'napa' (bread) in full as dental 'na' meets the broad lip release of stressed 'pa'. Final 'ko' closes farther back and rounds into a deeper chamber, an audible turn from bread towards the heated enclosure around it.
usage_notes: The verb directly takes the food, while nominal use covers baking. A surrounding phrase may identify the vessel or heat source. Exact temperatures and timed instructions stay with the source recipe when they matter.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: A communal oven gathered several households around one firing, with each loaf read by colour, smell, and the practised hand.

semantic_domains.activity: cooking food through surrounding dry heat
semantic_domains.creation: turning dough or another food into a baked result
semantic_domains.physical: heat reaching food from an enclosing chamber
